subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Social Media and Participatory Democracy
stimulus: Social media has changed the way many Americans communicate about politics. Develop an argument as to whether the use of social media has helped or hindered participatory democracy.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: First Amendment to the Constitution of the United States, Federalist No. 10, "Letter from a Birmingham Jail".)
parts.scoring_notes: Must take a clear position. Generic statements about social media changing communication do not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “Using social media helps participatory democracy because users can improve their political knowledge.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “By connecting people to multiple institutions and political processes, social media has helped participatory democracy in the United States.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “Social media has hindered participatory democracy by making Americans less certain about political information.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “When social media is used to spread negative information about politics, it makes citizens less willing to participate in the democratic process.”

wrong_examples: “Social media has improved or impeded democracy.” Do not respond to the prompt
common_traps: Restating the prompt without making a claim
Describing the issue without taking a position

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 3 points, must have earned Row A, and one piece of evidence must come from the First Amendment, Federalist 10, or Letter from Birmingham Jail.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (0 points): No accurate evidence or evidence not relevant to the topic.

required_elements: No accurate or relevant evidence
alternatives.correct_example: No points earned — evidence is inaccurate or irrelevant to the prompt.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (1 point): Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: Must provide one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.
alternatives.correct_example: Must provide one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.

common_traps: Evidence must be relevant but need not directly support a specific claim to earn 1 point

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (2 points): Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim/thesis OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence.

required_elements: Provide one piece of specific and relevant evidence that supports the claim or thesis. OR
alternatives.correct_example: Provide one piece of specific and relevant evidence that supports the claim or thesis. OR

required_elements: Must provide two pieces of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.
alternatives.correct_example: Must provide two pieces of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.

common_traps: To earn 2 points, the response must have earned Row A (claim/thesis)

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (3 points): Uses two pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim, one from a listed foundational document.

required_elements: Provide two pieces of specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis. One of these pieces of evidence must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt. The other piece of evidence can come from a different foundational document or from knowledge of course concepts. Examples that do not earn points: Provide evidence that is not specific
alternatives.correct_example: Provide two pieces of specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis. One of these pieces of evidence must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt. The other piece of evidence can come from a different foundational document or from knowledge of course concepts. Examples that do not earn points: Provide evidence that is not specific

required_elements: “Politics is about communication.” Provide evidence that is not relevant to the topic of the prompt
alternatives.correct_example: “Politics is about communication.” Provide evidence that is not relevant to the topic of the prompt

required_elements: “The Third Amendment stops the military from seizing a civilian’s house.” Examples of evidence that are relevant to the topic of the prompt:
alternatives.correct_example: “The Third Amendment stops the military from seizing a civilian’s house.” Examples of evidence that are relevant to the topic of the prompt:

required_elements: “Linkage institutions connect people to their representatives.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Linkage institutions connect people to their representatives.”

required_elements: “Political information includes new events and investigative journalism.” Examples of acceptable specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis (one example is one piece of evidence):
alternatives.correct_example: “Political information includes new events and investigative journalism.” Examples of acceptable specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis (one example is one piece of evidence):

required_elements: “Social media can be used to learn more about candidates and the election process.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Social media can be used to learn more about candidates and the election process.”

required_elements: “Presidents have used social media to encourage voters to support their policies.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Presidents have used social media to encourage voters to support their policies.”

required_elements: “Having freedom of speech means that people can complain to their representatives.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Having freedom of speech means that people can complain to their representatives.”

required_elements: “Misinformation on social media has led to violent reactions from citizens.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Misinformation on social media has led to violent reactions from citizens.”

required_elements: “The First Amendment guarantees that people have the right to communicate their needs to their elected representatives.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The First Amendment guarantees that people have the right to communicate their needs to their elected representatives.”

required_elements: “The First Amendment makes it easier for social media to distribute false information.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The First Amendment makes it easier for social media to distribute false information.”

required_elements: “Federalist No. 10 argues that factions are a threat to democracy.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Federalist No. 10 argues that factions are a threat to democracy.”

required_elements: “Letter from a Birmingham Jail urged citizens to take direct action to seek equality and justice.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Letter from a Birmingham Jail urged citizens to take direct action to seek equality and justice.”

common_traps: One piece must be from a foundational document listed in the prompt

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have provided at least one piece of specific and relevant evidence. Must explain the connection between evidence and the argument about participatory democracy.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Social media gives us better access to what’s happening in politics, and when we know what’s going on, we can act to make things better. That’s good for participatory democracy.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Social media makes it easier for our representatives to respond to citizens’ needs, which improves efficacy and increase participation further.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “If the First Amendment allows citizens to spread misinformation, the quality of political knowledge will continue to decrease, and people will participate less in politics.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “When social media intensifies political polarization, people can be discouraged from participating in the political process and may even tune out politics all together.”

common_traps: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B
Simply restating evidence without reasoning does not earn this point

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut or refute it. Merely acknowledging an alternate perspective without engaging with it does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “Even if social media has introduced people to questionable information, it has encouraged them to become involved in politics anyway, which is good.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “Even though the rise of ideologically driven news sources has created some confusion, they are also giving our elected representatives a better perspective on what the people want, and that is good for participatory democracy.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “While some may argue that social media improves democracy because it improves linkage institutions, it has also harmed the credibility of news sources, which is ultimately worse for participatory democracy.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “Many would argue that social media gives more people an opportunity to express their opinions to a larger audience, but that could lead to an overwhelming amount of information that can discourage people from wanting to engage in the political process in the first place.”

wrong_examples: “Some people say that linkage institutions are good for democracy.” Describe an alternate perspective but do not rebut or refute that perspective
“Changes in communication have given rise to ideologically-driven programming, which has contributed to issues with political knowledge and political participation.”
common_traps: Simply identifying an alternate perspective without rebutting/refuting it does not earn this point
